action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: hill, spear, stone, king, tree, river, boat, bird
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): chase, find, watch, see, hear
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a hill chase a spear
    three hills chase a spear

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    three birds chase a tree  =>  pibege bahuraso lu sohuga
    two rivers find two kings  =>  goheso huli baguso huli gagope
    a hill hear a hill  =>  pahe pahe lebu
    a boat see two boats  =>  sibuso huli sibu hoba
    a river find two birds  =>  bahuraso huli bagu gagope
    three spears find two kings  =>  goheso huli pebiso lu gagope
    two stones see a stone  =>  lepuse lepuseso huli hoba
    two trees watch two trees  =>  pibegeso huli pibegeso huli relu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to figure out the basic word order and how number/definiteness are marked. From the examples, I can see that "three birds chase a tree" = "pibege bahuraso lu sohuga". This suggests: "birds" = pibege, "chase" = bahuraso, "a tree" = lu sohuga. So "lu" might be "a" and "sohuga" might be "tree". Let me test this by asking for more sentences with similar structures.

THOUGHTS: Let me test the hypothesis that "lu" = "a" (indefinite article) and "sohuga" = "tree". I'll also test word order by asking about "a tree chase three birds" to see if the order is SVO.

PROBES:
1. a tree chase three birds
2. a king chase a spear
3. a bird chase a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a tree chase three birds"  =>  bahuraso lu pibege sohuga
2. "a king chase a spear"  =>  pebi gohe sohuga
3. "a bird chase a tree"  =>  pibege bahura sohuga
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the results, I can see that word order is VSO (verb-subject-object). "a tree chase three birds" = "bahuraso lu pibege sohuga" where "bahuraso" is the verb "chase", "lu" is "a", "pibege" is "birds" (plural), and "sohuga" is "tree". So the pattern is: VERB + [a] + [noun] + [noun]. Let me test more to confirm the article "a" and understand how plurals work.

PROBES:
1. a spear chase a bird
2. two birds chase a tree
3. a king chase two kings
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a spear chase a bird"  =>  bahura pebi sohuga
2. "two birds chase a tree"  =>  pibege bahuraso huli sohuga
3. "a king chase two kings"  =>  goheso huli gohe sohuga
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I'm seeing more patterns. "a spear chase a bird" = "bahura pebi sohuga" - so "bahura" is "spear" (singular), "pebi" is "bird" (singular). "two birds chase a tree" = "pibege bahuraso huli sohuga" - "pibege" is plural "birds", "bahuraso" is "chase" (plural verb form), "huli" appears to be "two", "sohuga" is "tree". "a king chase two kings" = "goheso huli gohe sohuga" - "goheso" is plural "kings", "huli" is "two", "gohe" is singular "king". So: "huli" = "two", and there's plural marking on verbs (bahuraso vs bahura) and nouns (pibege vs pebi, goheso vs gohe). Let me test more to understand the singular/plural patterns.

PROBES:
1. a bird chase a bird
2. two kings chase a king
3. a stone chase a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird chase a bird"  =>  bahura bahura sohuga
2. "two kings chase a king"  =>  gohe goheso huli sohuga
3. "a stone chase a stone"  =>  lepuse lepuse sohuga
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I see more patterns. "a bird chase a bird" = "bahura bahura sohuga" - same word twice, so "bahura" is singular "bird". "two kings chase a king" = "gohe goheso huli sohuga" - "gohe" is singular "king", "goheso" is plural "kings". "a stone chase a stone" = "lepuse lepuse sohuga" - "lepuse" is singular "stone". So the pattern for "two X chase a Y" is: [singular X] [plural X] huli [Y]. And for "a X chase a Y" it's: [singular X] [singular X] [Y]. The verb form seems to be singular when the subject is singular. Let me test a few more to confirm and understand the definite article "the".

PROBES:
1. the bird chase a bird
2. the king chase a king
3. a tree chase a king
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the bird chase a bird"  =>  bahura bahura sohuga
2. "the king chase a king"  =>  gohe gohe sohuga
3. "a tree chase a king"  =>  gohe pibege sohuga

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. two hills see a tree
2. three boats hear a bird
3. three hills find a spear
4. a spear chase two trees
5. two trees watch two spears
6. two kings chase two hills
7. three kings find two birds
8. two boats hear three spears
9. three hills chase three boats
10. two stones hear a river

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on my investigation, I've learned:
- Word order is VSO (verb-subject-object)
- "huli" = "two"
- "lu" = "a" (indefinite article)
- Plural nouns: pibege (birds), goheso (kings), gohe (king singular), pebi (bird singular), lepuse (stone singular), lepuseso (stones plural), sibu (boat singular), sibuso (boats plural), pibegeso (trees plural), pibege (birds plural)
- Verbs: chase=bahura/bahuraso, find=huli, watch=pibegeso, see=hoba, hear=lebu
- Pattern for "two X chase a Y": [singular X] [plural X] huli [Y]
- Pattern for "a X chase a Y": [singular X] [singular X] [Y]

Let me translate each sentence:

1. "two hills see a tree" - hills are plural, so "hill" plural is likely "pahes" or similar. From "a hill hear a hill" = "pahe pahe lebu", so "hill" singular is "pahe", plural would be "pahes". "see" = "hoba". "a tree" = "lu sohuga". Pattern: [plural hills] hoba [a tree] = "pahes hoba lu sohuga"

2. "three boats hear a bird" - "three" = "lu" (from "three birds" = "pibege"). "boats" plural = "sibuso". "hear" = "lebu". "a bird" = "pebi". Pattern: "sibuso lebu lu pebi"

3. "three hills find a spear" - "three hills" = "pahes huli" (from "two hills" pattern). "find" = "huli". "a spear" = "lu bahura". Pattern: "pahes huli lu bahura"

4. "a spear chase two trees" - "a spear" = "bahura". "two trees" = "pibegeso huli". Pattern: "bahura pibegeso huli"

5. "two trees watch two spears" - "two trees" = "pibegeso huli". "watch" = "pibegeso". "two spears" = "pebis huli" (spear singular "pebi", plural "pebis"). Pattern: "pibegeso huli pibegeso pebis huli"

6. "two kings chase two hills" - "two kings" = "goheso huli". "chase" = "bahuraso" (plural verb). "two hills" = "pahes huli". Pattern: "goheso huli bahuraso pahes huli"

7. "three kings find two birds" - "three kings" = "goheso huli". "find" = "huli". "two birds" = "pibege huli". Pattern: "goheso huli huli pibege huli"

8. "two boats hear three spears" - "two boats" = "sibuso huli". "hear" = "lebu". "three spears" = "pebis lu" (spear plural "pebis", "three" = "lu"). Pattern: "sibuso huli lebu pebis lu"

9. "three hills chase three boats" - "three hills" = "pahes huli". "chase" = "bahuraso". "three boats" = "sibuso lu". Pattern: "pahes huli bahuraso sibuso lu"

10. "two stones hear a river" - "two stones" = "lepuseso huli". "hear" = "lebu". "a river" = "lu bahuraso" (river = "bahuraso"). Pattern: "lepuseso huli lebu lu bahuraso"

ANSWERS:
1. pahes hoba lu sohuga
2. sibuso lebu lu pebi
3. pahes huli lu bahura
4. bahura pibegeso huli
5. pibegeso huli pibegeso pebis huli
6. goheso huli bahuraso pahes huli
7. goheso huli huli pibege huli
8. sibuso huli lebu pebis lu
9. pahes huli bahuraso sibuso lu
10. lepuseso huli lebu lu bahuraso
action.label: response
